title: Long Exhales Are Your Brake Pedal

hook.rwenLine: If activated states are the accelerator, long exhales are the brake. They're the one regulation tool that works in 30 seconds, anywhere, for anyone — and they cost nothing.
hook.culturalNote: Heart rate variability research: extending the exhale relative to the inhale activates the vagus nerve and shifts the body from sympathetic to parasympathetic dominance. The science is solid; the practice is older than the science.

chunks.native: Breathe in for 4 counts, out for 6 — the simplest pattern that works
chunks.literal: Coherent breathing protocols (Brown, Gerbarg) — ratio matters more than rate

chunks.native: Two short inhales through the nose, one long exhale through the mouth — the fastest reset known
chunks.literal: Andrew Huberman / Karl Deisseroth (Stanford) — most rapid stress-reduction technique observed
chunks.phonetic: Two in, one long out. Three seconds total.

chunks.target: It works whether you believe it or not
chunks.native: Long exhales are mechanical. The body responds even when the mind is doubtful.
chunks.literal: Polyvagal: vagal afferent activation is autonomic, not cognitive

pattern.name: Exhale longer than you inhale.
pattern.explanation: Any time you exhale longer than you inhale, you're applying the brake. 4-in, 6-out is the simplest. The physiological sigh (two short inhales, one long exhale) is fastest. You don't need silence, candles, or technique — you need the ratio.

examples.native: Physiological sighs until you stop counting.
One long exhale before you reply. Even one.

pairs.right: In the middle of a moment, no time

exercises.question: Why does exhaling longer than you inhale calm the body?

options.text: It activates the parasympathetic system — the brake on fight-or-flight
It distracts you from the stressor

exercises.explanation: Long exhales stimulate the vagus nerve, which slows heart rate and shifts the autonomic nervous system out of activation. It's mechanical, not motivational.

exercises.prompt: How do you do a physiological sigh?
correct: Two short inhales through the nose, one long exhale through the mouth
Double inhale through nose, exhale through mouth

exercises.translation: The simplest regulation tool there is.

exercises.question: Best moment to practise so the tool is there when you need it?

options.text: When already calm — so the body learns the pattern

exercises.explanation: Practise in calm so the body remembers the pattern when stress arrives. Tools you've used before are tools you reach for.

rwenDialogue.intro: Let's do three rounds together. Right now.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Breathe in for four. Out for six. I'll count.

userChoices.target: Done. I feel a small shift.
userChoices.native: Most people notice something after three rounds.
userChoices.feedback: Good. That shift is the parasympathetic system coming online. It's real.
Honest. The effect is cumulative — try it three times today and notice on the third.
Counting is a scaffold, not the practice. The practice is the longer exhale.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: This tool is in your pocket forever now. Use it in queues, before meetings, in the car. The body learns the pattern by repetition.

correct: Physiological sigh — two inhales, one exhale

mission.title: Today's mission
mission.task: Three times today — in queues, before any difficult moment, lying down before sleep — do three rounds of 4-in, 6-out. Or one physiological sigh. The tool only works if you've used it before.

phase8.scenario: User has just tried the breathing technique. Rwen helps them notice what shifted (or didn't) and where they could deploy it tomorrow.
phase8.rwenRole: A calm, factual companion. Not selling the technique — testing it with them.
phase8.successCriteria: User has identified one specific moment tomorrow where they'll try it.
